Sequence of Returns Simulator

Monte Carlo analysis of returns, inflation, and dynamic withdrawals.

Investment Horizon

Optional: model an uncertain end date. Each simulation picks a horizon between the endpoint minus/plus values; those bounds are treated as very likely (about 2-in-100 tails). Leave both at 0 for a fixed timeline.

000s
Advanced simulation settings

How many CPU cores to use when running simulations. Higher is faster but uses more of your computer while the run is in progress.

%

Averages a band of runs around each percentile (P5–P60) to reduce noise. 0 = a single run.

Primary metric for ranking (P5–P60), on-plan success, 3D coloring, and Goal Seek. Auto picks median/yr when a horizon range is set. The other metric is still shown alongside results.

Max spending shortfall vs. plan when scoring runs. Affects the “on plan” success rate and 3D chart coloring. Goal Seek uses its own Risk Tolerance when enabled.

Adds year-to-year persistence by drawing runs of consecutive years averaging this length (Historical Resampling and Smoothed Historical); set to 1 for fully independent years. Log-Normal uses it for year-to-year smoothing instead.

Softens Smoothed Historical's output so it isn't limited to only the exact years in your selected range. Not used by the other two methods.

How many simulations Goal Seek runs to score each candidate plan while searching. Lower = faster search but noisier results; higher = slower but more reliable. The final plan is always confirmed using the full Number of Simulations above.

Withdrawal Strategy

000s

In today's dollars — the simulator keeps purchasing power constant, so don't build in inflation raises.

Spending Over Time

Stage how your base withdrawal changes over retirement. Each tier's annual real change compounds continuously (negative = spend more now, less later); extra withdrawal adds a flat amount on top for that tier's years. Set any value to 0. Add tiers to stage different patterns — the last tier applies to all remaining years. Remove all but one tier for a flat schedule. When Goal Seek includes this section, it tunes only the first tier's extra withdrawal.

Minimum withdrawal

Always withdraw at least this amount each year (capped by portfolio balance), even if your schedule above would go lower — the chart below reflects this floor. Add tiers to stage different minimums over time — the last tier applies to all remaining years. Remove all tiers for no minimum. Doesn't apply to a Specific List.

Paste from a spreadsheet — new lines, tabs, commas, semicolons, or pipes. Negative values are deposits. Last value is reused for remaining years.

Minimum withdrawal

Always withdraw at least this % of that year's list amount (capped by portfolio balance), even if market/balance adjustments would cut lower — the chart below reflects this floor. Add tiers to stage different minimums over time — the last tier applies to all remaining years. Remove all tiers for no minimum.

Gifting

Add an extra gift withdrawal in years when your portfolio balance (after growth and your regular withdrawal or deposit) exceeds the tier's balance threshold and that year's spending fully met your plan — guardrails or penalties did not cut it short. Add tiers to stage different gifts over time — the last tier applies to all remaining years. Set Gift to 0 (or leave Balance blank) on early tiers to defer gifting to later years. Remove all tiers for no gifting. The chart below shows a dotted gift ceiling above your planned withdrawal.

Dynamic Adjustments & Guardrails

Market adjustment

Adjust withdrawal based on nominal market return (see chart). When balance is below the Low threshold, that adjustment always applies. When balance is above Expected or High, those adjustments only raise spending on bad market years — they never reduce it after a good year.

000s
Balance adjustment

Smoothly scale your total withdrawal based on portfolio balance: below the floor, spending ramps down toward the max cut (reached at $0); above the ceiling, it ramps up by the boost rate for every extra multiple of the ceiling — with no upper cap. Between the two, spending is unchanged.

Floor Balance Max Cut
000s
%

0 = no floor

Ceiling Balance Boost Rate
000s
%

0 = no ceiling

Glide-path spend-down

Actively spend surplus down toward a target ending balance. Each year the simulator computes the balance still needed to fund your remaining plan and land on the target at the horizon; when the portfolio sits above that glide path, the spend rate share of the surplus is withdrawn on top of the plan. The glide path declines toward the target as the horizon shrinks, so extra spending concentrates in the later years of comfortable paths and never cuts below your plan. Slide spend timing toward later to hold surplus back for late retirement — it raises the glide path so the early years stay invested, which helps plans that would otherwise dip to their minimum withdrawals mid-retirement. When Goal Seek includes this section, it sets the target from Goal Seek's Target Ending Balance and tunes the spend rate.

000s

blank = off; 0 = spend to zero

%

share of surplus spent each year

later balanced sooner

Goal Seek

Search for the highest sustainable spending plan instead of simulating fixed numbers. Check Include in search on settings below to choose what Goal Seek can tune.

Minimum acceptable chance the plan hits both targets below.

Max lifetime spending shortfall vs. plan in bad markets. 0% = stay on plan; higher = bigger plan, more belt-tightening allowed. Minimum withdrawals still protect essentials.

000s

Balance to leave behind at the end of the horizon, on top of never running out.

Explore specific paths

A 3D plot of representative paths from the 5th to 60th percentiles. Column height represents portfolio balance, color reflects the annual market return for that year (Red = crash, Green = boom). Drag to rotate, scroll to zoom, right-drag to pan. Single-click a column to pin that simulation and mouse over each year for values; click the column again or empty space to release. Double-click a column to explore ~200 nearby simulations; double-click again to return.

Sequence Risk: IRR vs Average Return

Each dot is one simulation. Dots below the dashed diagonal were hurt by early bad years — their money-weighted IRR trails their time-weighted average return; dots above it got lucky early. Paths in the lower-right had good average returns but still fell short of the plan: sequence risk in its purest form. Click a dot to see that path's withdrawals against its plan.

Average Timelines

Shows portfolio balance and dynamic withdrawals over time for the 60th through 5th percentile paths. Point size and shape reflect each year's nominal return.

Distribution of Real Returns

Summary of average annual real return across all simulations. Each stat also shows the IRR — the money-weighted return, which accounts for the timing of withdrawals and deposits.
